title: Dog Eyelashes Growing Inward: Causes, Risks, and Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's eyelashes are rubbing their eye, the risks of corneal ulcers, and why this medium-urgency condition requires a vet visit.
content_html:
What Is It?

When a dog's eyelashes grow toward the eye, it is typically caused by one of three conditions: Distichiasis, Ectopic Cilia, or Trichiasis. Distichiasis occurs when extra lashes grow from the oil glands along the eyelid margin. Ectopic Cilia involves hairs poking through the underside of the eyelid, while Trichiasis involves facial hair rubbing against the eye. In all cases, these hairs act like tiny needles, causing constant irritation and scratching the cornea.

Is It an Emergency?

This is considered a Medium Urgency condition. While it is not usually a systemic emergency, it is extremely painful for your pet and poses a high risk for corneal ulceration or secondary infections. If your dog is squinting, pawing at their eye, or if you see significant redness or cloudiness, you should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to prevent permanent vision damage.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's eye can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. High-resolution images allow the vet to see the position of the stray hairs and the condition of the cornea. This helps them determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and whether a specialized ophthalmology referral might be necessary.

faqs.question: Can I pluck the inward-growing eyelash at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pluck the hairs yourself. Plucking is temporary, painful, and often leaves behind a sharp, short stubble that can cause even more damage to the eye's surface.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of eye irritation from lashes?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive blinking or squinting, watery or mucoid discharge, redness of the whites of the eyes, and your dog rubbing their face against furniture or using their paws.

faqs.question: How are inward-growing eyelashes permanently treated?
faqs.answer: Vets typically use procedures like cryotherapy (freezing the follicles) or electrolysis to permanently destroy the hair follicle, preventing the lash from growing back.
